title: Projects
text:
Spatiotemporal Patterns of Outbreak-Related Illnesses


Analyzed 50 years of U.S. outbreak data using Shiny dashboards and spatial statistics.


 

Bayesian Beta-Carotene Supplementation Study


Analyzed nutrient interactions using a Bayesian mixed model framework.


 

Dietary Sulfamerazine Study on Brown Trout 


Modeling dose–response effects of dietary sulfamerazine on red blood Cell Counts in brown trout.


 

Forecasting Lung Cancer Mortality


Compared time series models (SES, HDES, ARIMA) for lung cancer prediction in the U.S.


 

Serum Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) Determinants in Prostate Study


A regression-based clinical assessment on serum Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA) determinants in prostate cancer.


 

Malaria Trend Analysis in Comoros


Modeling seasonal spikes in malaria using ARIMA modeling to inform timely response.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, M.S, Gstat

Thank you for visiting. I appreciate your time here
I am a Statistician and Ph.D. student in Biostatistics at the University of Washington. I completed my M.S. in Statistics at the University of New Mexico and my B.Sc. in Actuarial Science at the University of Ghana.
My research develops statistical methods for questions in health and population data, with an emphasis on work that is rigorous, practical, and relevant to clinical and policy decisions.
Teaching and mentoring matter to me as much as research. I teach and tutor students in quantitative coursework and mentor those from underrepresented backgrounds finding their footing in STEM. I am also actively involved with the American Statistical Association and ENAR (Eastern North American Region), where I have served as a session chair, reviewed for student research programs, and taken part in education and early-career initiatives.



Away from work, I like to cook (hopefully not burning anything while staring at the game), follow European football (yes, I argue about referees like it’s my job), and enjoy graphic design. I also love to travel and experience new cultures, which keeps me curious about the wider world and the different ways people make sense of it.

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, Gstat
text:
I am a graduate statistician working in spatial statistics, survival analysis, and statistical methods for public health. I am completing an M.S. in Statistics at the University of New Mexico and will begin a Ph.D. in Biostatistics at the University of Washington in September 2026. I hold a B.Sc. in Mathematical Science from the University of Ghana.
My work applies statistical methods to public health challenges, with interests in applied methodology, population health, and data-informed policy. My long-term goal is to build a research program in Ghana developing statistical methods for Africa’s health and education challenges. I value clear communication, rigorous analysis, and collaborative problem-solving.

title: Featured Posts
text:
Community and Mentorship

Diversity Mentoring Program: Building Connections and Transforming Careers
Amstat News – Stats from the Road — This article spotlights the ASA’s Diversity Mentoring Program (DMP), which brings together underrepresented minority statisticians and data scientists to build a supportive community. It describes how the 2024 program hosted 120 participants and how the 2025 JSM workshop will provide one‑on‑one mentoring, small‑group discussions, and networking sessions:contentReferenceoaicite:0. Participants note that DMP connections often lead to job opportunities and long‑term collaborations:contentReferenceoaicite:1. Emmanuel Kubuafor shares his own experience: “It has been an eye‑opener… very informative and resourceful”:contentReferenceoaicite:2.
Read the article

title: Featured Posts
text:
🏆 Lester R. Curtin Award — American Statistical Association (2025)
Emmanuel Kubuafor received the ASA’s Lester R. Curtin Award at the 2025 Joint Statistical Meetings in Nashville.
The award supports promising early-career health statisticians in attending professional conferences and advancing methodological skills.
Read the UNM announcement · About the award

🎓 Campersand: Math Summer Camp at UNM (2024)
As a counselor at UNM’s annual Campersand Math Camp, Emmanuel helped mentor over 100 middle- and high-school students in math, logic, and computing.
The program fosters mathematical curiosity and teamwork through engaging, project-based learning.
Read more
🤝 ASA Diversity Mentoring Program (2025)
Featured in Amstat News’s “Stats from the Road” series, Emmanuel shared his experience with the ASA Diversity Mentoring Program (DMP), which connects early-career statisticians from underrepresented backgrounds with professional mentors.
He described the program as “an eye-opener… very informative and resourceful.”
Read the article

Updated November 2025

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, Gstat
text:
What I Do
I develop and apply statistical methods that turn complex data into clear, defensible conclusions. My current work spans non-parametric estimation of spatial autocorrelation in neuroimaging residuals, time-varying hazard modeling in clinical survival data, and spatial methods for diagnosing learning gaps in education systems. I work primarily in R, with MATLAB for computational work and LaTeX for reproducible academic writing.

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, Gstat
text:
Research Interests

Spatial statistics and autocorrelation modeling
Survival analysis and time-varying hazards
Statistical methods for neuroimaging (fMRI cluster-level inference)
Population health and data-informed policy
Statistical methods education

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, Gstat
text:
Teaching
I have taught and supported a range of statistics courses as a course instructor and teaching assistant at the University of New Mexico and the University of Ghana, covering introductory statistics, statistical computing, biostatistics, regression analysis, and probability. I care about clear explanation and building genuine statistical intuition in students. See the Teaching page for details.

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor, Gstat
text:
Technical Skills

Statistical computing: R (tidyverse, spatial and survival packages), MATLAB
Methods: spatial autocorrelation, variogram estimation, survival/Cox modeling, bootstrap inference, systematic review
Reproducible research: LaTeX, Quarto, R Markdown, Git
Communication: academic writing, technical presentation, data visualization

title: Emmanuel Kubuafor
text:
Focus Areas

  
    Spatial Statistics
    Autocorrelation modeling and non-parametric variogram estimation, with
    applications to neuroimaging and education data.
  
  
    Survival Analysis
    Time-varying hazards, piecewise Cox models, and bootstrap validation for
    clinical and population-health questions.
  
  
    Statistics Education
    Teaching and methods development across introductory statistics,
    computing, biostatistics, regression, and probability.
